description: The provided image is severely blurred and completely unreadable, with no discernible features related to the investigation of cryogenic rolling effects on Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel. No microstructure micrographs, diffraction patterns, spectroscopic spectra, mechanical performance curves, or other materials characterization data can be identified from the visual input, so no direct scientific observations can be extracted from this image alone. No valid information supporting the study's conclusions on microstructure evolution, texture changes, or mechanical property trade-offs can be confirmed visually from the provided input.

This figure is a flow schematic illustrating the full processing route of Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel specimens for cryogenic rolling experiments. First, 100 mm × 100 mm forged billets are cut into plate-shaped specimens with thicknesses of 1.5 mm, 2.0 mm, 3.5 mm and 10.0 mm. All specimens are then subjected to solution treatment at 1000 °C for 1 h followed by water quenching. After that, a circulation processing cycle is carried out: specimens are first immersed in liquid nitrogen for cryogenic treatment, then rolled by working rolls for cryogenic rolling with ~2% thickness reduction per single pass, and re-immersed in liquid nitrogen after each pass, until cumulative thickness reductions of 30%, 50%, 70% and 90% are reached respectively for different test groups.

This set of optical microscopy (OM) images presents the microstructure evolution of low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C austenitic steel subjected to different cryogenic rolling (CR) thickness reductions. Subfigure (a) shows the undeformed sample, featuring uniform equiaxed austenite grains with an average size of ~40.3 μm and a small amount of annealing twins inside grains. Subfigures (b), (c), (d) correspond to samples with 30%, 50%, 70% CR reduction respectively: grains gradually elongate along the rolling direction (RD), slip band density inside grains rises with increasing reduction, slip band intersections are observed at 30% reduction, and grains turn to flattened banded morphology at higher reductions. Subfigures (e) and (f) correspond to 90% CR reduction: (e) shows the overall fibrous texture formed by heavily elongated grains, and (f) (high magnification with 1 μm scale bar) presents intersecting slip bands at fine scale. No deformation-induced martensitic transformation is observed across all samples. The figure provides direct metallographic evidence for CR-induced microstructure evolution, supporting the correlation between deformation degree, defect density, grain morphology and mechanical property changes of the test steel.

This group of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images presents the microstructure evolution of low-density Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C austenitic steel subjected to different cryogenic rolling (CR) thickness reductions. Subfigure (a) shows the undeformed sample with equiaxed austenite grains, clear grain boundaries, and dispersed dislocation lines. Subfigure (b) for 30% CR reduction reveals Taylor lattice structures formed by intersecting slip bands. Subfigure (c) for 50% CR exhibits dislocation walls and abundant deformation twins. Subfigure (d) for 70% CR shows dislocation cells and walls, with decreased deformation twin density. Subfigure (e) for 90% CR displays a fibrous deformed structure, with an inset selected area electron diffraction pattern of continuous rings confirming randomly oriented nanograins. Subfigure (f) is the corresponding dark-field TEM of 90% CR sample, with an inset grain size distribution chart giving an average grain size of 58.8 nm. Subfigure (g) is a bright-field TEM of 90% CR sample highlighting residual deformation twins. The figure demonstrates that increasing CR reduction induces progressive dislocation accumulation, microstructure transition from planar slip features to twinning, then to dislocation substructures and nanocrystalline grains without martensitic transformation, supporting the cryogenic deformation induced strengthening and grain refinement mechanism of the test steel.

This figure presents XRD characterization results of Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel under different cryogenic rolling (CR) conditions, including undeformed state and 30%, 50%, 70%, 90% thickness reduction. Subplot (a) shows XRD profiles: all samples only display characteristic diffraction peaks of austenite (γ(111), γ(200), γ(220), γ(311)), no deformation-induced martensite is observed, and peaks gradually broaden with increasing CR reduction. Subplot (b) shows the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the representative γ(220) peak, which increases from 0.496° for the undeformed sample to 1.022° after 90% CR, positively correlated with deformation level. Subplot (c) exhibits calculated microstrain (left y-axis, unit: 10^-3) and dislocation density (right y-axis, unit: 10^14 m^-2): both rise monotonically with CR reduction, with microstrain increasing from 2.8×10^-3 to 7×10^-3, and dislocation density rising from 3.2×10^14 m^-2 to 23.6×10^14 m^-2. This figure confirms that the test steel maintains single austenite phase during CR, and XRD peak broadening is attributed to grain refinement, elevated microstrain and dislocation density rather than phase transformation.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) inverse pole figure (IPF) orientation maps and matching {100}, {110}, {111} pole figures for Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C low-density austenitic steel under three cryogenic rolling (CR) deformation states. Subfigure (a) corresponds to the undeformed sample (scale bar 50 μm), showing equiaxed austenite grains with random crystallographic orientation. Subfigure (b) corresponds to the sample with 30% CR thickness reduction (scale bar 20 μm), exhibiting partially elongated grains and the formation of copper {112}<111> texture. Subfigure (c) corresponds to the sample with 90% CR reduction (scale bar 5 μm), showing heavily fragmented, fibrous grains elongated along the rolling direction, with texture transformed to brass {110}<112> type. The results demonstrate that increasing CR deformation progressively refines grains, elongates microstructure along the rolling direction, and drives systematic texture evolution, which links to the improved strength and reduced ductility of deformed samples, supporting that CR effectively modulates the microstructure and texture of the studied low-density steel.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) grain boundary distribution maps and misorientation statistics of Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel under different cryogenic rolling (CR) conditions. Subfigure (a) shows the undeformed sample (scale bar 50μm), with black lines representing high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs) and red lines representing low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs), dominated by HAGBs. Subfigure (b) is the 30% CR sample (scale bar 20μm), with visibly increased LAGBs. Subfigure (c) is the 90% CR sample (scale bar 5μm), showing a high density of fine LAGBs and fibrous refined grain structure. Subfigure (d) is the misorientation angle frequency histogram: undeformed samples have a sharp peak at ~60° (twin boundaries, ~60% frequency), while the proportion of LAGBs (<10°) rises to 89.4% after 30% CR and further to 94.1% after 90% CR. The results demonstrate that increasing CR deformation significantly promotes LAGBs formation, reflecting continuous accumulation of deformation substructures and grain refinement, which contributes to the enhanced strength of the steel.

This group of figures presents Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps and statistical results of Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel under different cryogenic rolling (CR) deformation degrees, characterized by electron backscatter diffraction. Subfigure (a) shows the undeformed sample with a 50μm scale bar, dominated by blue color indicating extremely low local misorientation and negligible initial stored strain. Subfigure (b) displays the 30% CR sample with a 20μm scale bar, where green and yellow regions emerge primarily near prior grain boundaries, verifying that deformation preferentially concentrates at grain boundaries under low strain. Subfigure (c) presents the 90% CR sample with a 5μm scale bar, containing large fractions of green, yellow, and red high-misorientation regions distributed in rolling bands, demonstrating remarkable strain accumulation after heavy deformation. Subfigure (d) is the frequency distribution histogram of average KAM angle: the undeformed sample has a sharp peak below 0.5°, the 30% CR sample shows a broad distribution centered around 1°, and the 90% CR sample exhibits a distribution shifted to higher angles up to 5°. These results confirm that local misorientation, which correlates with dislocation density and stored strain, increases monotonically with CR reduction, supporting the evolution law of strain accumulation, dislocation proliferation and grain refinement during cryogenic rolling of the test steel.

This figure displays the mechanical properties of Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel treated with different cryogenic rolling (CR) thickness reductions (undeformed, 30%, 50%, 70%, 90%). Subfigure (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves for all samples: the undeformed specimen shows the highest elongation (~60%) and lowest peak stress (~882 MPa), while CR samples show progressively increased tensile strength and decreased failure strain with rising CR reduction. Subfigure (b) quantifies the evolution of four mechanical indicators as functions of CR deformation: ultimate tensile strength increases from 882 MPa to 1861 MPa, yield strength from 505 MPa to 1791 MPa, microhardness from 284 HV to 578 HV, while elongation drops from 64.5% to 6.6% as CR reduction rises to 90%. This figure demonstrates a clear strength-ductility trade-off of the steel during cryogenic rolling, where higher deformation enhances strength and hardness at the cost of ductility, driven by deformation-induced microstructural evolution including dislocation accumulation, grain refinement and deformation twinning.

This set of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images presents the tensile fracture morphology of Fe-28Mn-8Al-1C low-density steel, with a unified 10 μm scale bar for all subgraphs. Subgraph (a) corresponds to the undeformed sample, (b) to 30% cryogenic rolling (CR) thickness reduction, (c) to 50% CR reduction, (d) to 70% CR reduction, and (e) to 90% CR reduction. The undeformed sample shows large, deep dimples, a typical feature of ductile fracture. With increasing CR reduction, dimples become gradually smaller and shallower; the 90% CR sample exhibits extremely fine dimples and partial quasi-cleavage features, indicating a transition from fully ductile fracture to ductile-brittle mixed fracture. This morphological evolution matches the trend of tensile elongation decreasing from 64% (undeformed) to 6.6% (90% CR), supporting the strength-ductility trade-off effect induced by cryogenic rolling deformation.
